definition: Recovering gradually from illness or medical treatment; in a state of returning to health.
example: The convalescent patient was encouraged to take short walks to rebuild his strength.
mnemonic: CONVALescent — 'conval' from Latin 'convalescere' (to grow strong again).

definition: A progressive increase in intensity, force, or volume reaching a peak.
example: Public outrage built to a crescendo after the scandal was revealed.
mnemonic: CRESC- means 'grow' in Italian; think of a music swell growing louder.
confusableNote: Often misused to mean the peak itself; technically it is the buildup toward the peak.

definition: To dry out completely, removing all moisture; to deprive of vitality.
example: The relentless desert heat desiccated the once-lush vegetation within days.
mnemonic: DE-SICC-ate: 'sicc' from Latin siccus (dry) — de-dry something completely.

definition: To take apart piece by piece or systematically destroy a structure or system.
example: The new administration moved swiftly to dismantle the previous era's regulatory framework.
mnemonic: DIS-MANTLE: remove the 'mantle' (covering/structure) piece by piece.

definition: To swell or expand outward from internal pressure, especially beyond normal limits.
example: Malnutrition caused the children's abdomens to distend painfully.
mnemonic: DI-STEND: 'stend' from Latin tendere (to stretch) — stretch outward in all directions.
confusableNote: distend vs. extend: distend implies abnormal swelling outward; extend simply means to lengthen.

definition: Temporarily inactive or in a suspended state, with potential for future activity.
example: The volcano had been dormant for centuries before it suddenly erupted.
mnemonic: DORM-ant: like sleeping in a dorm — 'dormir' means to sleep in Latin/French.

definition: Gradually decreasing in size, number, or strength; steadily diminishing.
example: The dwindling reserves of fresh water alarmed environmentalists worldwide.
mnemonic: DWINDLE sounds like 'dwindle down' — shrinking toward nothing.

definition: To overshadow or cause to seem inferior; to surpass and render another obscure.
example: Her later achievements eclipsed the early success that had first made her famous.
mnemonic: Like a solar eclipse, one thing blocks and obscures another completely.

definition: To give rise to or bring about a feeling, situation, or condition.
example: Years of broken promises engendered deep distrust between the two communities.
mnemonic: EN-GENDER: to 'give birth to' something — from Latin generare (to beget).

definition: To happen or occur afterward as a consequence or result.
example: After the controversial verdict, heated protests ensued across the capital.
mnemonic: EN-SUE: think 'in sequence' — it comes right after something else.
confusableNote: ensue vs. issue: ensue means to follow as a consequence; issue can mean to emerge or a problem.

definition: To completely destroy or eliminate something, especially something harmful, so it no longer exists.
example: Global health campaigns succeeded in eradicating smallpox by the late twentieth century.
mnemonic: E-RADIC-ate: 'radic' from Latin radix (root) — pull out by the roots entirely.

definition: Former; belonging to an earlier time; previously holding a certain status.
example: The erstwhile rivals surprised everyone by announcing a joint business venture.
mnemonic: ERST-while: 'erst' is an archaic word for 'formerly' — 'erstwhile' = 'at a former time.'

definition: To increase rapidly in intensity, magnitude, or scope.
example: What began as a minor dispute quickly escalated into a full diplomatic crisis.
mnemonic: ESCAL-ate: like climbing an escalator — steadily rising higher.

definition: Quickly fading or disappearing; lasting only a brief time; transitory.
example: The evanescent beauty of cherry blossoms makes their brief season all the more treasured.
mnemonic: EVAN-escent: 'evan' from Latin evanescere (to vanish) — evaporating into nothing.

definition: To call forth or bring to mind a feeling, memory, or image.
example: The old photograph evoked a wave of nostalgia for his childhood summers.
mnemonic: E-VOKE: 'voke' from Latin vocare (to call) — call something out from within.
confusableNote: evoke vs. invoke: evoke means to call forth (a feeling/memory); invoke means to call upon (authority/law).

definition: To expel or eliminate something troubling or malevolent; to rid oneself of a harmful influence.
example: Writing her memoir helped her exorcise the demons of a difficult childhood.
mnemonic: EX-ORC-ise: 'ex' (out) + force the bad spirit out — casting out evil.
confusableNote: exorcise vs. exercise: exorcise means to drive out evil/trouble; exercise means physical activity or to practice.

definition: Still in existence; not destroyed, lost, or extinct.
example: Only three extant manuscripts of the medieval poem have survived to the present day.
mnemonic: EX-TANT: 'ex' (out/standing) + 'tant' — still standing out there, not gone.
confusableNote: extant vs. extent: extant means still existing; extent refers to the range or scope of something.

definition: Inactive, dormant, or uncultivated; (of land) left unplanted to recover fertility.
example: After years of frantic productivity, she let her creative work lie fallow for a season.
mnemonic: FALLOW field: farmers leave fields 'fellow-less' (without crops) to rest and recover.

definition: New and inexperienced; just beginning to develop or emerge.
example: The fledgling startup struggled to compete against established industry giants.
mnemonic: FLEDG-ling: a baby bird just growing feathers (fledge) — barely able to fly yet.

definition: To instigate or stir up trouble, rebellion, or discord; to incite unrest.
example: The demagogue used inflammatory rhetoric to foment resentment among the populace.
mnemonic: FO-MENT: think 'ferment' — like fermenting trouble, making it bubble and brew.

definition: Just begun and not fully developed or organized; rudimentary and incomplete.
example: Her inchoate ideas about social reform had yet to coalesce into a coherent platform.
mnemonic: IN-CHO-ate: 'in' (not yet) + 'choate' (complete) — an inchoate plan is not yet whole.

definition: To stir up or urge someone to act, especially in a violent or harmful way.
example: The fiery speech was designed to incite the crowd to march on the government building.
mnemonic: IN-CITE: 'cite' from Latin citare (to set in motion) — set someone in motion.

definition: To bring about or cause something to happen; to persuade someone to do something.
example: The promise of tax credits induced many companies to invest in renewable energy.
mnemonic: IN-DUCE: 'duce' from Latin ducere (to lead) — lead someone into doing something.

definition: To bring about or initiate an action or event, often something negative.
example: Investigators determined that a single agitator had instigated the violent confrontation.
mnemonic: IN-STIG-ate: 'stig' from Latin instigare (to goad) — prod or poke someone into action.

definition: To overwhelm with an excessive amount; to flood or submerge completely.
example: After the product launch, the company was inundated with customer complaints.
mnemonic: IN-UND-ate: 'und' from Latin unda (wave) — waves crashing in, flooding everything.

definition: To give strength, energy, or vitality to; to animate and stimulate.
example: A brisk morning run invigorated her and sharpened her focus for the day ahead.
mnemonic: IN-VIGOR-ate: put 'vigor' (strength/energy) into something.

definition: A temporary pause or calm period in an otherwise active or turbulent situation.
example: During the lull in fighting, aid workers rushed in supplies to the besieged city.
mnemonic: LULL: sounds like a lullaby — quiet, calm, things have slowed to sleep.

definition: Just coming into existence and beginning to develop; emerging in early stages.
example: The nascent democracy faced enormous challenges in establishing stable institutions.
mnemonic: NASC-ent: from Latin nasci (to be born) — just being born, barely started.

definition: A person who is new to a subject, skill, or belief; a beginner or novice.
example: As a neophyte investor, she made several costly mistakes before learning market fundamentals.
mnemonic: NEO-PHYTE: 'neo' (new) + 'phyte' (plant) — a newly planted seedling, just starting to grow.

definition: No longer in use or relevant; outdated and superseded by something newer.
example: The rise of digital streaming rendered the video rental store obsolete almost overnight.
mnemonic: OB-SOL-ete: 'solete' from Latin obsolescere — 'ob' (against) + solete (accustomed), no longer the norm.

definition: Having been something in the past but no longer so; former.
example: The onetime champion now struggled to qualify for regional competitions.
mnemonic: ONE-TIME: it happened just once, in the past — not anymore.

definition: To cause something, especially something undesirable, to happen suddenly or prematurely.
example: The assassination precipitated a chain of events that led to the outbreak of war.
mnemonic: PRE-CIPIT-ate: 'cipit' from Latin caput (head) — headlong rush, things fall forward fast.
confusableNote: precipitate (v) vs. precipitous: precipitate means to cause hastily; precipitous means extremely steep or rash.

definition: Present in large amounts; produced or existing in great quantities; lavishly abundant.
example: She offered profuse apologies after inadvertently offending her colleague.
mnemonic: PRO-FUSE: 'fuse' from Latin fundere (to pour) — pouring forth in great quantity.

definition: To increase rapidly in number or amount; to spread and multiply abundantly.
example: Social media platforms have proliferated to the point of dominating daily communication.
mnemonic: PROLIF-erate: 'prolif' from Latin proles (offspring) + ferre (to bear) — bearing many offspring rapidly.

definition: To spread or transmit widely; to reproduce and disseminate ideas, organisms, or signals.
example: Misinformation propagates rapidly through social networks, often outpacing corrections.
mnemonic: PROPAG-ate: from Latin propagare (to extend by layering a plant) — making more and spreading out.

definition: Spreading or increasing unchecked and unrestrained; flourishing excessively.
example: Corruption was rampant in the agency, with bribes expected at every level.
mnemonic: RAM-pant: like a ram charging without restraint — wild and uncontrolled.

definition: To restore to a full or sufficient supply; to fill up again what has been depleted.
example: After the drought, autumn rains finally replenished the depleted reservoir.
mnemonic: RE-PLEN-ish: 'plen' from Latin plenus (full) — make full again.

definition: To reduce costs, expenditures, or staff; to cut back as a means of economizing.
example: Facing falling revenues, the company retrenched by laying off a quarter of its workforce.
mnemonic: RE-TRENCH: to dig back into your trench — retreat and reduce your exposure.

definition: Promoting health and well-being; conducive to a healthy or pleasant environment.
example: Physicians recommended the salubrious mountain air as a remedy for her respiratory ailment.
mnemonic: SALUBRI-ous: from Latin salus (health) — full of health benefits.
confusableNote: salubrious vs. salutary: salubrious refers to physically healthy conditions; salutary means beneficial, often morally.

definition: To cut off or divide sharply; to put an end to a connection or relationship completely.
example: The two nations severed diplomatic ties following the border incident.
mnemonic: SEVER: sounds like 'severe' — a sharp, decisive, complete cut.

definition: An excessive amount of something; an overabundant supply beyond what is needed.
example: A surfeit of candidates applied for the single fellowship position.
mnemonic: SUR-FEIT: 'sur' (over) + 'feit' from French faire (to do/make) — overdone, too much made.

definition: Remaining as a trace or remnant of something that was once greater or more fully developed.
example: The human appendix is considered a vestigial organ with no clear modern function.
mnemonic: VESTIG-ial: 'vestig' from Latin vestigium (footprint/trace) — just a faint trace left behind.

definition: To decrease in strength, intensity, or extent; to diminish gradually.
example: Public enthusiasm for the new policy waned as its costs became apparent.
mnemonic: WANE: opposite of wax — the moon wanes (shrinks) after it is full.
confusableNote: wane vs. wax: wane means to decrease; wax means to increase or grow.

definition: Harshly irritating in manner; tending to cause offense through bluntness or rudeness.
example: His abrasive personality alienated colleagues who might otherwise have supported his proposals.
mnemonic: ABRAS-ive: like sandpaper that ABRADES (scrapes) — rough and irritating to others.
